---
title: "Writing in Quarto"
subtitle: "Markdown, Citations, and Manuscript Formatting"
---

## Purpose

This chapter covers writing in Quarto independently of any data or analysis. This is relevant if you want to write any part of your manuscript — including the results section — directly in Quarto, rather than copying output into a separate Word or LaTeX document.

## Text formatting

Quarto uses Markdown for text formatting:

| Markdown | Output |
|---|---|
| `**bold**` | **bold** |
| `*italic*` | *italic* |
| `***bold italic***` | ***bold italic*** |
| `` `code` `` | `code` |
| `~~strikethrough~~` | ~~strikethrough~~ |
| `H~2~O` | H~2~O |
| `R^2^` | R^2^ |

For APA 7, statistical symbols should be italicised. Use `*...*` in Markdown:

| Statistic | Markdown | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | `*M* = 310.8` | *M* = 310.8 |
| SD | `*SD* = 155.9` | *SD* = 155.9 |
| t-test | `*t*(48) = 2.31` | *t*(48) = 2.31 |
| p-value | `*p* < .001` | *p* < .001 |

## Headers

Use `##` for first-level sections within a chapter, `###` for subsections. The chapter title is set in the YAML `title:` field:
````markdown
## Methods
### Participants
#### Inclusion criteria
````

::: callout-tip
In a Quarto book, avoid using `#` (H1) inside chapter files — it is reserved for the chapter title set in the YAML.
:::

## Lists and callouts
````markdown
- unordered item
  - nested item

1. ordered item
   1. nested item
````

Callout boxes draw attention to key points:
````markdown
::: callout-note
A note — good for asides and tips.
:::

::: callout-warning
A warning.
:::

::: callout-important
An important point.
:::
````

## Equations

Write LaTeX math inline with `$...$` or in a display block with `$$...$$`:
````markdown
The model was specified as:

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{Tense}_i + \beta_2 \text{Lifetime}_i + \varepsilon_i
$$ {#eq-model}
````

Reference in prose with `@eq-model` → "Equation 1".

## Cross-references {#sec-crossref}

Cross-references keep figure, table, and section numbers in sync automatically. Every label must start with the correct prefix:

| Type | Prefix | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Figure | `fig-` | `@fig-rt` |
| Table | `tbl-` | `@tbl-desc` |
| Section | `sec-` | `@sec-results` |
| Equation | `eq-` | `@eq-model` |
````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-rt
#| fig-cap: "Total reading time by condition."
ggplot(df, aes(x = lifetime, y = tt)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```
````
````markdown
As shown in @fig-rt, reading times differed by condition.
````
````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-desc
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics by condition."
tbl3 |> gt()
```
````
````markdown
Descriptive statistics are presented in @tbl-desc.
````

## Numbered example sentences {#sec-examples}

Pandoc's `(@)` syntax auto-numbers linguistic examples continuously across the document:
````markdown
(@cond-a) Der Dirigent hat den Komponisten *gekannt*, 
          der die Sinfonie geschrieben hat.
(@cond-b) Der Dirigent hat den Komponisten *gekannt*, 
          der gestorben ist.

The stimuli in (@cond-a) and (@cond-b) differed in the lifetime
status of the referent.
````

::: callout-tip
For sub-examples (1a, 1b), interlinear glosses, or full linguistic formatting in PDF output, use the `linguex` or `gb4e` LaTeX packages via `include-in-header` in your YAML.
:::

## Citations {#sec-citations}

Add the following to your document YAML:
````yaml
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
````

Download `apa.csl`:
````r
download.file(
  "https://www.zotero.org/styles/apa",
  here::here("apa.csl")
)
````

Citation syntax:

| Syntax | Output |
|---|---|
| `@Bates2015` | Bates et al. (2015) |
| `[@Bates2015]` | (Bates et al., 2015) |
| `[-@Bates2015]` | (2015) |
| `[@Bates2015, p. 12]` | (Bates et al., 2015, p. 12) |
| `[@Bates2015; @R2024]` | (Bates et al., 2015; R Core Team, 2024) |

Getting BibTeX entries:

- **Zotero** — export as BibTeX (recommended)
- **Google Scholar** — Cite → BibTeX
- **DOI** — `doi2bib.org`
- **R packages** — `toBibtex(citation("lme4"))`

Add a references section at the end of your document:
````markdown
## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
````

## Inline reporting {#sec-inline}

Embed R values directly in prose so they update automatically:

````markdown
The sample comprised ` r n_distinct(df$px)` participants.
The mean total reading time was ` r round(mean(df$tt, na.rm = TRUE), 1)` ms
(*SD* = ` r round(sd(df$tt, na.rm = TRUE), 1)`).
````

For model results, define a helper function in `R/utils.R`:
````{r}
#| eval: false
report_t <- function(model, term) {
  b   <- broom::tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE)
  row <- b[b$term == term, ]
  glue::glue(
    "*b* = {round(row$estimate, 2)}, ",
    "95% CI [{round(row$conf.low, 2)}, {round(row$conf.high, 2)}], ",
    "*t*({round(broom::glance(model)$df.residual)}) = {round(row$statistic, 2)}, ",
    "*p* {ifelse(row$p.value < .001, '< .001', paste0('= ', round(row$p.value, 3)))}"
  )
}
````

````markdown
The effect of lifetime was significant (` r report_t(m1, "lifetimeDead")`).
````

::: callout-tip
Any number that appears in your manuscript should come from your data or models, not typed manually. This prevents inconsistencies when data changes.
:::

## Exercises

### Exercise 1 — Text formatting

Write a short methods paragraph (3-5 sentences) in your exercise `.qmd` that includes:

- At least one italicised statistic in APA style (*M*, *SD*, or *p*)
- One piece of inline R code reporting a value from `df`
- One callout box with a note about your data

### Exercise 2 — Citations

1. Add `bibliography: references.bib` and `csl: apa.csl` to your YAML
2. Create a `references.bib` file with at least two entries (use `toBibtex(citation("lme4"))` and one from Google Scholar)
3. Cite both references in prose using `@key` and `[@key]` syntax
4. Add a references section at the end of your document and render to HTML

### Exercise 3 — Cross-references

1. Add a figure and a table to your document with `#| label:` and `#| fig-cap:` / `#| tbl-cap:`
2. Reference both in prose using `@fig-` and `@tbl-`
3. Add a section label `{#sec-methods}` to a heading and cross-reference it

### Exercise 4 — Numbered examples

Write two example sentences from your own stimuli using `(@)` syntax and cross-reference them in a prose sentence below.

### Exercise 5 — Inline reporting

Complete the following sentence using inline R:
````markdown
The sample comprised ... participants (*N* trials = ...).
Mean total reading time was ... ms (*SD* = ...).
````

## Chapter slides
````{=html}
<a href="../slides/07_writing.html" class="btn btn-outline-primary" target="_blank">
  Open slides ↗
</a>
````
